title: Biology Unit 2 - Cells, Development, Biodiversity and Conservation

questions.question: The diagram shows a prokaryotic cell, drawn by a student.

parts.text: Which labelled structure is a plasmid?
parts.answer: B

parts.text: Which labelled structure enables this cell to adhere to other bacterial cells in conjugation?
parts.answer: D

parts.text: State how ribosomes in prokaryotic cells are different from those in eukaryotic cells.
parts.answer: They are smaller / 70S (eukaryotic are 80S)

parts.text: Prokaryotic cells may also have a capsule and a flagellum. State one function of each of these structures.
parts.answer: Capsule: protection / prevents desiccation / adhesion / immune evasion; Flagellum: movement / locomotion

questions.question: The diagram shows three structures that form after the fertilisation of an egg cell.

parts.text: Explain why the cells in the morula are genetically identical.
parts.answer: They are produced by mitosis; no crossing over / genetic recombination; no independent assortment; each cell receives identical genetic material to the zygote

parts.text: Structure A can be used as a source of pluripotent stem cells. Name structure A.
parts.answer: Blastocyst

parts.text: Stem cells can be used as a medical treatment when a patient has had a severe heart attack. A severe heart attack can cause the death of heart muscle cells. The medical treatment involves injecting stem cells into damaged areas of the heart muscle. Suggest why injecting pluripotent stem cells may benefit a person who has had a severe heart attack.
parts.answer: Pluripotent stem cells can differentiate into heart muscle cells / cardiomyocytes; they can replace damaged tissue / repair heart muscle; can restore function / contractility

questions.question: Some of the characteristics of an organism are due to polygenic inheritance.

parts.text: The photograph shows some Chinese white kidney beans. The mass of individual Chinese white kidney beans varies. This is partly due to polygenic inheritance. State what is meant by the term polygenic inheritance.
parts.answer: A characteristic controlled by two or more genes; each gene contributes to the phenotype; results in continuous variation

parts.text: Which of the following graphs represents the distribution of mass of these beans?
parts.answer: B

parts.text: The inheritance of height is another example of polygenic inheritance. It is possible to predict how tall children could become when they reach adulthood. The prediction uses the following steps: 1. Add the mother's height (cm) to the father's height (cm). 2. Add 13 cm if the child is a boy or subtract 13 cm if the child is a girl. 3. Divide by two. A woman with a height of 1.59 m has a daughter. The father is 1.76 m tall. Calculate the predicted adult height of their daughter.
Explain why their daughter may not grow to be this height.
parts.answer: Environmental factors affect height (nutrition, diet, exercise, health); the prediction is only an estimate / not exact; genetic variation / recombination; mutation; other genes may be involved

questions.question: Tendrils are produced by climbing plants, such as pea plants. The photograph shows the tendrils on a growing pea shoot. A tendril is a modified leaf that can coil around anything it touches. Cells in the tip of the tendril divide by mitosis.

parts.text: A student investigated the mitotic index of cells in the tips of tendrils. Acetic orcein stain was added to the tissues from the tips of the tendrils. Give one reason why the cells were stained in this investigation.
parts.answer: To make chromosomes visible / to identify stages of mitosis / chromosomes are normally colourless

parts.text: The photograph shows these cells, as seen using a light microscope. Which labelled cell is in the metaphase stage of mitosis?
parts.answer: D

parts.text: The table shows the results of this investigation. Calculate the mitotic index.
parts.answer: Mitotic cells = 15 + 3 + 10 + 5 = 33; Total cells = 117 + 15 + 3 + 10 + 5 = 150; Mitotic index = 33/150 = 0.22

parts.text: Suggest the advantages of tendrils for the pea plant.
parts.answer: Allows plant to climb / gain height; access more light for photosynthesis; provides support and reduces risk of damage / collapse; helps compete with other plants

parts.text: Pea plants produce flowers. Some cells in the flowers undergo meiosis. Explain how the separation of chromosomes in meiosis gives rise to genetic variation in the offspring of pea plants.
parts.answer: Independent assortment of homologous chromosomes in meiosis I produces different combinations; separation of sister chromatids in meiosis II produces genetically different gametes; fertilisation is random so different gametes combine to give variation

questions.question: The photograph shows a Madagascan pygmy kingfisher (Ceyx madagascariensis).

parts.text: This kingfisher is endemic to forests in Madagascar. State what is meant by the term endemic with reference to this kingfisher.
parts.answer: It is found only in Madagascar / restricted to that region

parts.text: State the domain in which this kingfisher is classified.
parts.answer: Eukaryota

parts.text: A scientist recently collected new data about the classification of kingfishers, using molecular evidence. The data were used to propose the reclassification of some kingfishers, including Ceyx madagascariensis. The diagrams show an old classification and the proposed reclassification of five species of kingfishers. Using the proposed reclassification, explain which species of kingfisher are most closely related to Ceyx madagascariensis.
parts.answer: Alcedo leucogaster and Alcedo cristata; they share the most recent common ancestor with Ceyx madagascariensis on the proposed tree

parts.text: Explain how molecular evidence led to the reclassification of species and how the scientific community would have reviewed the evidence before it was accepted. Use the information in the photograph and the diagrams to support your answer.
parts.answer: Molecular evidence (e.g. DNA/base sequence or amino acid sequence comparisons) can be used to determine relatedness; more similar sequences indicate a more recent common ancestor. This can show that species that look similar may not be most closely related, and that Ceyx madagascariensis may be closer to Alcedo species than to other Ceyx species. Scientists would publish methods and results; other scientists would review (peer review) and replicate/verify analyses; conclusions accepted only after scrutiny of data, reliability, sample size, and agreement with other evidence.

questions.question: The photograph shows kenaf plants growing on a farm in Nigeria. Kenaf is grown mainly in parts of Africa and southern Asia. Fibres from the stems of these plants are used to make paper. Fibres are obtained from the xylem and the phloem.

parts.text: The diagram shows a transverse section through the stem of a plant. Label the diagram to show the position of the xylem and the phloem.
parts.answer: Xylem on the inner side of each vascular bundle; phloem on the outer side

parts.text: Below are some statements about xylem and phloem. How many of these statements are correct?
parts.answer: A

parts.text: In an investigation, three rows of kenaf plants were grown in separate containers in a glasshouse. The temperature, light intensity, soil quality and soil quantity were controlled. The diagram shows how each row was treated. The table shows the means and standard deviations of the measurements of the fibres extracted from the plants at the end of this investigation. Calculate the percentage decrease in the mean diameter of the fibres from plants in row 2 compared with those from plants in row 1.
Comment on the results of this investigation. Use the information in the table to support your answer.
parts.answer: Water stress reduced fibre size. Row 2 (water reduced after 1 month) had the smallest mean length (0.64 mm), diameter (21.62 μm) and wall thickness (2.85 μm) compared with row 1 (0.91 mm, 26.95 μm, 3.44 μm). Row 3 values were intermediate (0.81 mm, 26.48 μm, 3.23 μm), suggesting stress later has less effect. Differences are larger than the standard deviations, supporting a real effect.

parts.text: The walls of xylem vessels contain cellulose. Which of the following shows the structure of part of a cellulose molecule?
parts.answer: B

questions.question: The photograph shows an electron micrograph of a cell. This cell is a beta cell from a human pancreas. Beta cells produce and secrete insulin.

parts.text: Which labelled structure produces ribosomes?
parts.answer: B

parts.text: Describe how insulin is transported from the rough endoplasmic reticulum to the cell surface membrane and secreted from the cell.
parts.answer: Insulin is synthesised on ribosomes attached to RER and enters the RER lumen; it is transported in vesicles to the Golgi apparatus; in the Golgi, it is modified and packaged into secretory vesicles; vesicles move to the cell surface membrane (by motor proteins on microtubules); vesicle fuses with membrane (exocytosis) and insulin is released outside the cell

parts.text: Explain why beta cells can produce insulin but red blood cells cannot.
parts.answer: Beta cells contain the gene for insulin and have the necessary regulatory factors to express it; they have RER and Golgi to synthesise and process proteins; red blood cells are enucleated (no nucleus) so lack the insulin gene and any genes for protein synthesis machinery; red blood cells are specialised for oxygen transport and have lost most organelles

parts.text: Beta cells contain many mitochondria. Explain how the mitochondria provide energy for the production and secretion of insulin.
parts.answer: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP; ATP is required for active transport (e.g. moving ions across membranes); ATP is needed for vesicle formation, movement (motor proteins) and fusion during exocytosis; ATP powers protein synthesis on ribosomes

questions.question: The photograph shows a river and mangrove trees in Bhitarkanika National Park in India.

parts.text: The biodiversity of this area of Bhitarkanika National Park could be measured by calculating species richness. Which of the following describes species richness in Bhitarkanika National Park?

options.text: the number of organisms in each of the species

parts.answer: D

parts.text: Describe one other way in which the biodiversity of this area of Bhitarkanika National Park could be measured.
parts.answer: Calculate species diversity index (e.g. Simpson's index) which accounts for richness and evenness; or measure evenness / relative abundance using quadrats/transects

parts.text: Saltwater crocodiles are found in the rivers in this area. The numbers of this endangered species have decreased over the past 40 years. Explain the possible causes of this decrease.
parts.answer: Habitat loss / degradation (deforestation, mangrove removal, river pollution); hunting/poaching; reduced prey availability; human-wildlife conflict; climate change affecting nesting success

parts.text: Scientists are involved in conservation projects to increase the numbers of endangered saltwater crocodiles, without causing a reduction in the genetic diversity of future populations. One project involves collecting the eggs from nests in different parts of Bhitarkanika National Park. The eggs are incubated at a suitable temperature. The incubation temperature influences the sex of the developing crocodiles. The graph shows the effect of temperature on the percentage of crocodiles that hatch as male. Calculate the number of female crocodiles that would develop from 350 eggs incubated at 32°C.
parts.answer: At 32°C, about 25% hatch as male, so 75% are female; females = 0.75 × 350 = 262.5 ≈ 263

parts.text: Explain how this project could increase the number of crocodiles rapidly, without reducing the genetic diversity of future populations in Bhitarkanika National Park. Use the information in the graph to support your answer.
parts.answer: Collecting and incubating eggs increases survival compared with natural nests (reduces predation / flooding), so more hatchlings are released. Use incubation temperatures that produce more females (e.g. 32–33°C gives low % males, so more females) to boost breeding population quickly. To maintain genetic diversity, collect eggs from many nests in different parts of the park (many parents), avoid selecting eggs from just one area, and release offspring back into the population to increase effective population size and reduce inbreeding.
